1: Nahum prophesied ca. 663-612 BCE — after the fall of Thebes (663 BCE, mentioned in Nahum 3:8) and before the fall of Nineveh (612 BCE, which Nahum predicts). He is the companion prophecy to Jonah: Jonah brought Nineveh to repentance (ca. 760 BCE) and YHWH relented; Nahum announces that Nineveh's subsequent return to violence and oppression means its final destruction is now inevitable. The contrast is instructive: divine patience has a limit; the same attributes of mercy and justice that led YHWH to spare Nineveh in Jonah's day lead him to destroy it in Nahum's day. Nineveh was destroyed in 612 BCE by a coalition of Babylonians and Medes, exactly as Nahum predicted.
2: 'A jealous and avenging God is YHWH; YHWH is avenging and wrathful; YHWH takes vengeance on his adversaries and keeps wrath for his enemies.' The opening characterization of YHWH is triple-emphatic: El qanna (jealous God — Exodus 20:5; Nahum 34:14), YHWH naqom (YHWH avenging — three times in this verse and the next), ba'al chemah (master of wrath). The repetition is not redundancy but liturgical intensity. Exodus 34:6-7, the great YHWH self-disclosure, lists both his mercy and his wrath; Nahum's opening foregrounds the wrath side — the side that Jonah feared would not be applied to Nineveh, but now, a generation later, will be.
3: 'YHWH is slow to anger and great in power, and YHWH will by no means clear the guilty.' The phrase erech apayim (slow to anger) appears in Exodus 34:6 — the same formula Jonah cited in frustration (Jonah 4:2). But here Nahum pairs it with lo yenaqqeh yenaqqeh (will by no means acquit/clear). The patience of YHWH does not cancel his justice; it amplifies it: 'His way is in whirlwind and storm, and the clouds are the dust of his feet.' The divine warrior imagery (storm-god theophany) establishes YHWH as a cosmic military power before the specific oracle against Nineveh begins.
4: 'He rebukes the sea and makes it dry; he dries up all the rivers; Bashan and Carmel wither; the bloom of Lebanon withers.' The drying of sea and rivers echoes the Exodus (Red Sea) and Jordan-crossing — YHWH's power over water as the signature of his saving and judging acts. Bashan (lush plateau east of Galilee), Carmel (the Galilean promontory famous for fertility), and Lebanon (famous for its cedars) are the bywords of the ancient Near East's most abundant landscapes. If YHWH's approach withers even these, Nineveh — built on a dry steppe — has no natural advantage.
5: 'The mountains quake before him; the hills melt; the earth heaves before him, the world and all who dwell in it.' Theophanic earthquake imagery (cf. Psalms 18:7; Nahum 97:5; Judges 5:5; Amos 4:13) — YHWH's approach causes seismic response. The universalizing scope ('the world and all who dwell in it') ensures that the oracle against Nineveh is not merely geopolitical but cosmic in its theological frame: YHWH, not Ashur, is the sovereign of the whole earth.
6: 'Who can stand before his indignation? Who can endure the heat of his anger? His wrath is poured out like fire, and the rocks are broken into pieces by him.' The rhetorical questions — who can stand? who can endure? — anticipate the answer: no one. The language of fire and rock-shattering appears in Jeremiah 23:29 (YHWH's word as fire that shatters rock) and Ps 46 (earth gives way, mountains fall into the sea). Assyria's famous engineering feats — its walls, gates, and bastions — are implicitly measured against the God who shatters rock, and found inadequate.
7: 'YHWH is good, a stronghold in the day of trouble; he knows those who take refuge in him.' The pivot verse of Nahum 1 — from wrath (Nahum 1:2-6) to goodness. YHWH's character is both the terror of his enemies and the safety of his people. The ma'oz (stronghold/refuge) is YHWH himself, not a city wall or military alliance. 'He knows (yodea) those who take refuge in him' — the divine knowledge is personal and protective, contrasting with the anonymity of mass destruction in Nahum 1:5-6.
8: 'But with an overflowing flood he will make a complete end of the adversaries, and will pursue his enemies into darkness.' The flood (shtetef) imagery is almost certainly playing on Nineveh's actual geography: Nineveh sat between the Tigris and Khoser rivers, and ancient accounts of the city's fall (Diodorus Siculus) mention that flooding breached its walls. Whether Nahum knew this specifically or whether the flood metaphor was later confirmed by history, the image is apt. 'Into darkness' — Sheol, annihilation, the absence of life and light.
9: 'What do you plot against YHWH? He will make a complete end; trouble will not rise up a second time.' The direct address to Nineveh's plotting minds. The divine counter-plot is total: kalah (complete end) — the same word used of Nineveh's total destruction in Nahum 1:8. The assurance that trouble will not rise up a second time is the promise of finality — Assyria will not recover from this blow to threaten again. This is eschatological: the specific historical judgment participates in the logic of YHWH's ultimate and irreversible victory.
10: 'For they are like entangled thorns, like drunkards as they drink; they are consumed like stubble fully dried.' Three similes of the enemy's vulnerability: entangled thornbushes (quick and complete burning), drunkards (incapacitated, unable to fight), and dry stubble (instant combustion). The thorns-and-stubble metaphor is common in biblical judgment poetry (Isaiah 5:24; Nahum 10:17; Nahum 33:11-12; Psalms 83:13). The Assyrian military machine, which had devastated the ancient world for over a century, is reduced to brushwood before YHWH's fire.
11: 'From you came one who plotted evil against YHWH, who counseled wickedness.' The one who plotted evil against YHWH (yoshev ra'ah al-YHWH yoetz beliya'al) from Nineveh likely refers to Sennacherib (705-681 BCE), who invaded Judah and demanded Jerusalem's surrender (2 Kgs 18-19). His field commander Rabshakeh's speech mocked YHWH explicitly: 'Do not let your God in whom you trust deceive you' (2 Kings 18:29-30; Nahum 19:10-13). The counselor of worthlessness (beliya'al) set himself against YHWH and will now bear the consequences.
12: 'Thus says YHWH, though they are at full strength and many, they will be cut down and pass away. Though I have afflicted you, I will afflict you no more.' The oracle now pivots to direct address to Judah: YHWH's affliction of his people (through Assyrian oppression) was purposeful and temporary. The empire at its full strength and many (shelimim vechen rabbim) is cut down — the harvest metaphor. The promise 'I will afflict you no more' is the comfort that Nahum's name announces.
13: 'And now I will break his yoke from off you and will burst your bonds apart.' The yoke and bonds of Assyrian tributary domination — literal and political — will be snapped. The yoke metaphor is specific: vassal states paid tribute and were bound by Assyrian hegemony; Judah under Ahaz and Manasseh paid heavy tribute to Assyria. The coming freedom is described in terms of physical release from these political-economic shackles.
14: 'And YHWH has given commandment about you: no more shall your name be perpetuated; from the house of your gods I will cut off the carved image and the metal image. I will make your grave, for you are vile.' The annihilation of Nineveh extends to its divine cult: the carved and metal images of Ashur, Ishtar, and the Assyrian pantheon will be destroyed. The name will not be perpetuated — name perpetuation was the ancient goal of monumentalism and empire-building. YHWH's decree ends both the dynasty and the cult.
15: 'Behold, upon the mountains, the feet of him who brings good news, who publishes peace! Keep your feasts, O Judah; fulfill your vows, for never again shall the worthless pass through you; he is utterly cut off.' The herald of good news running over the mountains to announce the enemy's defeat recalls Isaiah 52:7 (quoted in Romans 10:15) — the same image of the messenger's feet as the vehicle of salvation news. Judah can celebrate its festivals in peace; the tribute caravans of Assyria will no longer pass through. This verse serves as the hinge between the theological introduction (Nahum 1:1-14) and the specific battle-poem (Nahum 2).
